data.title: Buffett's rare stock purchases before retirement have backfired. What signal does this send?
data.description:
For the three years before his retirement, Buffett was a net seller of stocks. The few new positions he finally acquired have all suffered consecutive setbacks since 2026. For example, UnitedHealth Group's stock price plummeted by 20% in a single day.

Today, POOL, which Buffett had been continuously increasing his holdings in, also suffered a setback.

Now, the small opportunities that Buffett sees as potential are not opportunities at all, but rather traps.It's no wonder he's been hoarding so much cash.

Because the entire US stock market is now like AI, built on a foundation of "circular financing"... What happened? Berkshire Hathaway's investment in Pool Corporation began in the third quarter of 2024. In that quarter, Berkshire first disclosed its newly established position in Pool to the SEC, purchasing 404,057 shares. Based on market trading data, the average closing price of Pool stock that quarter was approximately $345.95. Entering the fourth quarter of 2024, Berkshire Hathaway continued its steady investment plan, increasing its holdings by 194,632 shares, a 48.17% increase from the end of the previous quarter, bringing its total holdings to 598,689 shares. In the first quarter of 2025, POOL's stock price declined significantly, and Berkshire Hathaway significantly increased its holdings by 865,311 shares, a substantial increase of 144.53%, rapidly raising its total holdings to 1,464,000 shares. In the second quarter of 2025, POOL's stock price further broke down, falling below the $300 mark. Berkshire Hathaway executed its largest single-quarter purchase since inception: regulatory filings show that Berkshire Hathaway increased its holdings by a staggering 1,994,885 shares in the second quarter of 2025, a surge of 136.26% quarter-over-quarter. This brought Berkshire Hathaway's total holdings in POOL to a record high of 3,458,885 shares, making it one of the company's top 25 holdings and giving Berkshire Hathaway ownership of 8.24% of POOL's total outstanding shares. After four consecutive quarters of buying and maintaining its holdings unchanged in the third quarter of 2025, Berkshire Hathaway's strategy underwent a subtle reversal at the end of 2025. An SEC 13F filing for the fourth quarter of 2025, disclosed in early 2026, revealed that Berkshire reduced its position in POOL for the first time, selling 390,000 shares, representing 11.28% of its total holdings at that time. It is noteworthy that this reduction occurred in an environment of extremely low stock prices. In the fourth quarter of 2025, POOL's average closing price was only $259.34, fluctuating between $228.75 and $312.05. Before reducing its position, Berkshire Hathaway's weighted average cost basis for its Pool Corporation holdings was approximately $320. Having just made a large-scale investment, Berkshire Hathaway drastically reduced its position at a loss, clearly "admitting its mistake." The latest financial report from Pool Corporation seems to confirm this decision: On February 19, 2026, Pool Corporation announced its fourth-quarter and full-year 2025 results, showing revenue, earnings per share (EPS), and forward guidance all falling short of Wall Street expectations, triggering a sharp sell-off. The stock price once fell to a new 52-week low of $216.32, already 30% below Buffett's cost basis. What does this mean? POOL's competitive position remains relatively solid: POOL's gross margin improved by 70 basis points to 30.1% in the fourth quarter, while its full-year underlying gross margin also improved by 20 basis points (a strong gross margin is one of the key indicators of strong competitiveness in the industry), and the company is still expanding: by the end of 2025, POOL's sales center network will expand to 456 directly operated outlets, up from 448 at the end of 2024. However, the company's revenue and profits have not yet stopped declining: net sales for the full year of 2025 were $5.289 billion, continuing to decline from $5.311 billion in 2024; net profit for the full year of 2025 further decreased by 6.4% to $406.4 million, and diluted earnings per share (EPS) continued to fall from $11.30 to $10.85. This is mainly due to continued inflationary pressure: sales and administrative expenses increased by 4% year-on-year to $992.3 million. With declining revenue, this increase in expenses significantly compressed the operating profit margin. Even worse is the deterioration in cash flow quality: operating cash flow plummeted 44% from $659.2 million in 2024 to just $365.9 million in 2025. Free cash flow (excluding capital expenditures) also fell from approximately $600 million to approximately $310 million. POOL's deteriorating performance, balance sheet, and even cash flow and prospects, while possessing characteristics inherent to its industry, also reflect the fragility of the current US economy as a facet of discretionary consumption: POOL's weakness is more easily understood within the context of the increasingly "top-heavy" US consumption structure: According to Moody's Analytics, the top 10% of earners in the US now contribute approximately 49% of all consumer spending—the highest level since this proportion began being tracked in 1989. Meanwhile, the bottom 80%'s share of consumption has fallen to a record low of only 41%. Now, only 10-20% of Americans can be considered truly middle class, enjoying a consumption proportion commensurate with their income level and number. Because the United States is a consumption-driven economy, its economy is now heavily influenced by the spending power of the wealthy. It is estimated that the top 10% of earners alone contribute about one-third of GDP. As for the bottom 80% of earners, their spending growth over the past six years has barely kept pace with inflation—meaning their standard of living has essentially stagnated since the start of the pandemic. Meanwhile, the US Gini coefficient is at a 60-year high, and the confidence gap between high- and low-income groups has widened to its widest level in over a decade. If the spending of the upper-middle class and wealthy, represented by pools, is shrinking, cracks are appearing in other areas as well: A survey in the third quarter of 2025 found that 20% of wealthy and high-net-worth individuals plan to reduce their spending on designer fashion; Bank of America (BofA) credit card data shows that US luxury goods spending has declined for 10 consecutive quarters. Of course, compared to superficial phenomena like pool and luxury goods spending, the real source of influence on the spending of wealthy Americans is the US stock market: the power supporting the consumption of wealthy Americans mainly comes from the "wealth effect" brought about by the stock market—the top 20% of US household wealth holds (directly and indirectly) approximately 87% of corporate stock assets. This has created a disturbing dependency: once the stock market experiences a prolonged correction of more than 10%, the impact will precisely target the group with the highest concentration of consumer spending power. Therefore, the overall US economy is now somewhat like AI's "circular financing": stock market rises – wealth effect – wealthy people consume – boost corporate profits and the economy – stock market continues to rise… However, this prosperity, solely maintained by the wealth effect and fiscal stimulus, will one day collapse, and the performance of discretionary consumer companies has already flashed warning signs.

data.title: The next step in AI: Intelligent Agents
data.description:
Authors: Liu Jin, Yang Yan, Du Runsheng

Human intelligence has three aspects: information collection, information processing to generate cognition, and action based on cognition. Currently, the main application of large language models is in chatbots like ChatGPT, with capabilities concentrated in the first two aspects. However, more useful machine intelligence goes beyond "understanding" and "speaking." If it can "do things" for us like a skilled individual or a group of talented people, it can obviously create greater value. This requires AI agents.

An agent is AI that can do things. LLM is the most important development in the field of AI in recent years. It has already demonstrated extraordinary capabilities in language understanding and generation, dialogue interaction, and knowledge integration, but it is a "brain in a vat"—good at thinking, analyzing, and answering questions, but unable to actually do things.

An agent is AI that can do things.

In the real world, most cognitive activities go beyond simply "providing answers"; they require a complete "cognition-action" loop. We demand that AI autonomously break down complex needs, plan processes, and utilize tools and resources to achieve a complete cycle from perception to decision-making to execution. Furthermore, we hope that AI's actions can extend beyond the realm of computers and the internet, performing tasks for us in the physical world. This requires AI to perceive signals from the physical world, engage in embodied thinking, and translate decisions into execution through devices/robots, directly impacting the real environment. To illustrate, an LLM (Limited Learning Model) is like "Zhuge Liang before he entered the world," skilled in analysis, engaging in strategic discussions with Liu Bei using the "Longzhong Plan," but limited to theoretical discussions. An intelligent agent, on the other hand, is like "Zhuge Liang after he entered the world," possessing comprehensive intelligence, strategizing, organizing resources, deploying troops, and personally leading an expedition to the north. Intelligent agents, using cutting-edge models like LLMs as their brains, are engineered through software to perform complex tasks driven by higher-order goals. It can be said that most complex AI applications in the future will use agents as their carriers. In fact, the AI ​​images we see in science fiction, such as Jarvis in *Iron Man* or HAL 9000 in *2001: A Space Odyssey*, are the creators' intuitive imagination of future AI using agents as carriers. However, AI that interacts with the physical world is extremely important and complex, and this part is now customarily discussed separately in the field of embodied intelligence/robotics. The Composition of Intelligent Agent Capabilities To exert significant value in applications across various industries, an ideal intelligent agent needs to possess several key conditions. First, intelligent agents should possess strong goal understanding and planning capabilities to demonstrate the autonomy of intelligence. Ideally, humans only need to provide an abstract goal, and the intelligent agent can understand the goal, break down the task, plan actions, and complete the execution loop with minimal human intervention. Like the machine TARS in the movie *Interstellar*, in an emergency, it can autonomously assess the situation, formulate and adjust action strategies based on the goal of "saving the astronauts," and even make decisions to sacrifice its own data to complete the mission. This requires machine intelligence to have deep "understanding/thinking" abilities (reasoning, planning, decision-making), the ability to make sharp decisions, and the ability to dynamically adjust task planning based on execution results and environmental feedback, rather than rigidly executing a predetermined path. Second, we hope that intelligent agents can "take action": execution and interaction capabilities. This means that it can use tools, perform operations, and interact directly with the external environment. In the digital world, intelligent agents can simulate human operations, completing tasks through keyboard input and screen clicks; they can also invoke tools through other programs or system interfaces; and they can leverage model strengths to achieve goals by writing and executing code. In the physical world, intelligent agents need to connect to various control systems and devices, manipulating physical objects by issuing commands and translating intelligent decisions into real-world actions. Third, we hope that intelligent agents possess excellent memory and learning capabilities. Memory and state management capabilities are prerequisites for completing long-term, complex tasks. In consumer-facing scenarios, such as personalized schedule management or long-term service support, intelligent agents need to remember user preferences, historical interactions, and long-term states across sessions to reduce repetitive communication and improve service quality. In enterprise-level applications, such as cross-cycle project management and complex business process advancement, intelligent agents need to remember task progress, intermediate results, and key decision-making basis to ensure the continuity of tasks during long-term, multi-stage execution and prevent deviation from the established goals. The significance of learning ability lies in our desire for intelligent agents to continuously improve, evolving from novices to experts through experience and knowledge absorption, much like human employees. Finally, intelligent agents also need strong reliability and controllability to generate value at scale. This is reflected both in the agent's ability to complete tasks stably and robustly, and in whether its behavior consistently aligns with human intentions and values. In *2001: A Space Odyssey*, the AI's choice to sacrifice human crew members to complete the mission is an extreme consequence of the objective function failing to effectively align with human values. As the intelligence and autonomy of intelligent agents increase, the risks of such alignment failures may be further amplified. How to Build Intelligent Agents? The basic logic is simple: using the most "intelligent" and ideal model available as the core (brain), a system is built through software engineering to compensate for the model's shortcomings and approximate the ideal form of an intelligent agent as closely as possible. Large models are currently the optimal choice for the brain of an intelligent agent because their trillions of parameters compress the massive amounts of knowledge accumulated by humans, possess powerful pattern recognition and generation capabilities, serve as a universal interface for processing various unstructured data, including language, and have good generalization capabilities, forming the foundation for handling various tasks. Furthermore, next-generation inference models, represented by OpenAI o1/DeepSeek R1, further propel the development of intelligent agents: enhanced reasoning capabilities lead to stronger task decomposition and planning, better self-checking and error correction, and allow intelligent agents to use tools more accurately. Large models have some structural weaknesses that directly limit the application value of intelligent agents in real-world business scenarios. Therefore, a core task of intelligent agent engineering is to use engineering methods to address these shortcomings, set boundaries, and constrain behavior around the model. First, large models themselves are not very reliable: they suffer from persistent illusions, outdated knowledge, and often irrational task breakdown and planning, lacking systematic verification mechanisms for specific tasks. As a result, the usability of intelligent agents built upon them is significantly reduced: the agent pushes the model from "dialogue" to "action," and errors are no longer just incorrect answers but potential operational risks. Real-world business tasks are often cross-system and long-chain; a small error can amplify at each stage, leading to a persistently high failure rate for long-chain tasks (for example, with a 95% success rate for a single step, the overall success rate for a 20-step chain is only about 36%). To address this, agent engineering typically employs several methods to enhance the reliability of large models by adding an "exoskeleton": introducing retrieval and knowledge bases (RAGs) to mitigate the impact of illusions and outdated knowledge; pre-designing and constraining workflows, rather than allowing completely free "autonomous agents," to limit acceptable execution paths; identifying and filtering high-risk outputs through multiple responses, self-consistency checks, or cross-validation between models; and implementing human approval at critical nodes to ensure that humans have the final say on high-risk actions. Secondly, large models have inherent limitations in their memory capacity: while they "memorize" a vast amount of knowledge during training, they do not continuously learn or "memorize" new knowledge during use after training. During each inference, they can only rely on a finite-length context window to "memorize" information about the current task (different models have different upper limits; content exceeding the window will be forgotten), and cannot naturally maintain stable, long-term individual memory like humans. However, in real-world business scenarios, we need machine intelligence with strong memory capabilities. For example, an AI teacher needs to continuously remember students' learning history, weaknesses, and preferences to truly achieve "personalized instruction" in subsequent explanations and exercises. To address these memory deficiencies, intelligent agent engineering typically employs the following enhancement methods: building an external memory bank to store user preferences, business knowledge, and historical interactions, allowing the intelligent agent to retrieve relevant information on demand through a retrieval mechanism, thus addressing long-term memory deficiencies; summarizing and compressing excessively long contexts, retaining core information, and freeing up token space to address short-term memory forgetting caused by information overload. Besides addressing these shortcomings, because intelligent agents need to act and interact with the environment, they require perception and execution. Firstly, large models themselves cannot actively perceive; they can only passively respond to input. Intelligent agents need to use external perception components to actively acquire environmental information. For tasks in the digital world, agent engineering can be used to create time-based triggers to periodically check logs, emails, stock price fluctuations, etc.; or event-based subscriptions and listening to receive event notifications pushed by APIs, or automatically waking up and recording data when database changes occur. In the physical world, agents can also collect visual, auditory, and tactile signals through devices such as sensors, cameras, and microphones. Secondly, large models lack inherent execution capabilities and require agent engineering to translate intentions into actual actions. Tool invocation is currently the most mainstream approach. Large models generate structured function call instructions based on task requirements, which are then parsed and executed by the intelligent agent framework, such as calling a weather API, querying a database, or sending an email. Another approach is to simulate human operation, using visual recognition and simulated actions like "looking at the screen, clicking buttons, and filling out forms" to complete tasks. The recently popular Doubao phone uses this method to perform intelligent agent operations. For more complex tasks, intelligent agents can also be configured with code interpreters (Code Interpreter/Sandbox), allowing the model to run programmed code, which greatly expands the boundaries of intelligent agent actions. The current capabilities of intelligent agents are largely defined by the year 2025, which has just passed and is often referred to by AI practitioners and tech media as the "Year Zero of Intelligent Agents." This observation is quite accurate. Thanks to the maturity of the following conditions, the development of intelligent agents has entered a fast track in the past year or so: First, the continuous progress of large models, mainly reflected in the emergence of inference models that provide stronger task understanding and planning capabilities, and the development of multimodal models that provide a foundation for intelligent agents to process and generate more complex information. Secondly, the maturity of infrastructure and ecosystem, including open-source frameworks such as LangChain and AutoGPT, has formed a standardized development paradigm after two years of iteration, greatly shortening the development cycle; the popularization of low-code/no-code platforms such as Dify and Coze allows business personnel who do not understand code to quickly generate a dedicated intelligent agent by dragging and dropping; it is worth mentioning that the MCP (Model Context Protocol) and skills (skill system) released by Anthropic in 2025 have provided important standards and inspiration for the intelligent agent ecosystem: MCP, as an open-source protocol standard, makes the interaction between large models and external data sources or tools more unified and convenient, while Skills packages the capabilities/workflows required by humans to complete a certain type of task, allowing agents to work more stably on such tasks. Although the technical content is not high, it has strong practicality at present. Furthermore, both academia and industry have invested significant talent and resources in the field of intelligent agents. For example, at ACL (Association for Computational Linguistics) 2025, over 230 papers were related to intelligent agents, the highest number ever, covering areas such as planning, tool usage, multi-agent collaboration, and evaluation. Driven by improved capabilities, a robust ecosystem, and increased resource investment, various industries are attempting to truly utilize intelligent agents. According to McKinsey's 2025 Global Survey, approximately 62% of surveyed organizations have experimented with agents in some of their business operations (23% have deployed agents at scale in at least one scenario, and 39% are pilot applications). However, looking at specific adoption data across business functions, the industry's application of agents is still in its early stages. The survey indicates that the functions most frequently using agents are, in order, IT, knowledge management, marketing, and service. Taking IT as an example, only 2% and 8% of surveyed companies have fully scaled and scaled their agent deployments, respectively, while 6% and 7% have piloted and experimented with agent deployments. This low level of scaled application is due to two main reasons: first, the capabilities of agents, as discussed earlier, are rapidly improving, but still far from full practicality; second, enterprise users across various industries need certain conditions to effectively utilize agents. Firstly, besides applications in data analysis, generation, and retrieval for short task chains, the large-scale application scenarios of intelligence can be broadly categorized into two types. One is in the programming field, where programming serves as the ideal "training ground" for intelligent agents. It offers environmental isolation, high fault tolerance, clear objectives, compatibility with current planning capabilities, executable programs, and immediate execution feedback. This makes it the first large-scale, commercial breakthrough for intelligent agents. Secondly, specialized intelligent agents in various industries (sales, customer service, human resources, etc.) can be grouped into a large category, sharing a common characteristic: currently, they are primarily workflow automation types. This is actually a stopgap measure to address the insufficient deep understanding (planning, decision-making) capabilities of intelligent agents. By reducing the openness of the agent's tasks, providing reference workflows, and defining a limited set of usable tools, the quality of the agent's work in these tasks can be improved. Further large-scale application of intelligent agents requires their capability evolution to bring tangible value to enterprises. Secondly, for enterprises to effectively utilize intelligent agents, organizational and resource alignment is crucial. According to Anthropic's latest survey in 2026, 46% of respondents indicated that integration with existing systems was the main obstacle to intelligent agent deployment; 43% and 42% cited implementation costs and data accessibility/quality, respectively; and 40% and 39% cited security/compliance and employee learning costs/resistance as significant obstacles (SMEs are particularly concerned about learning costs, with 51% of respondents pointing to this). While cost, security, and technological advancements are largely related to enterprise concerns, data issues, integration problems, and learning/talent issues all require improvement through organizational transformation, further digitalization, and company-wide learning. Summary and Outlook In the future, intelligent agents will be the primary vehicle for applying artificial intelligence across various industries and scenarios. It is foreseeable that with advancements in model capabilities and agent engineering, along with improvements in enterprise data governance and organizational adaptation, intelligent agents will gradually become highly competitive digital employees for every enterprise, competing and collaborating with human employees. From a path perspective, as mentioned earlier, the current large-scale application of intelligent agents focuses on programming and workflow automation. With the improvement of machine intelligence's deep understanding, it is expected that the application of intelligent agents will continuously expand its boundaries, capable of undertaking more abstract and complex tasks, and making more autonomous planning and decisions to transform human intentions into results. Of course, breakthroughs do not mean abandoning workflows. In high-risk enterprise scenarios, workflows/permissions/auditing will become "guardrails" to limit the action space of intelligent agents and ensure application security. For a considerable period, human approval and auditing may remain indispensable in the closed loop of intelligent agent work. Another key direction for the development of intelligent agents is to become human "personal assistants" or "intelligent agents." Compared to other types of intelligent agents, this direction has a higher barrier to entry because it requires stronger personalization capabilities, long-term memory, cross-scenario generalization capabilities, and stricter security boundaries. Personal assistant-type intelligent agents have the potential to reshape the way humans interact with the world—whether it's shopping, socializing, or information acquisition—and may have a disruptive impact on the business logic of many industries. The launch of the "Doubao Phone" in the past year, and the explosive popularity of "OpenClaw" in early 2026, have given us some exciting early explorations of assistant-type intelligent agents. The former represents an attempt at device-level intelligent agents: it deeply embeds intelligent agent capabilities into the operating system, simulating human operations to invoke various apps and complete tasks for users. The latter is an always-on solution that can proactively observe and execute tasks, and acquire rich personalized data and software control through local deployment, initially demonstrating some core characteristics of personal assistant-type agents. According to IDC's forecast, the number of active intelligent agents will climb from approximately 28.6 million in 2025 to 2.216 billion in 2030. This means that in five years, the number of digital workers capable of helping businesses or individuals perform tasks will be nearly 80 times what it is now, with a compound annual growth rate of 139%; the number of tasks performed will surge from 44 billion in 2025 to 415 trillion in 2030, with a compound annual growth rate of 524%; and token consumption will skyrocket from 500 billion in 2025 to 1.5 trillion in 2030, with a compound annual growth rate of 34 times. IDC's predictions may not be accurate, but the trend is very clear, and every company should prepare for it.

data.title: Has Trump overplayed his hand? The US Supreme Court rules global tariffs unconstitutional.
data.description:
The shoe has finally dropped! Just now (February 20, 2026), the U.S. Supreme Court issued its final ruling on Trump's most controversial "global tariff" policy since taking office: illegal, and overturned. This legal tug-of-war, which dragged on for months, ended with a temporary setback for the White House. 1. What happened? The core of this ruling is very clear: the president cannot raise taxes at will. The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that the global tariffs imposed by the Trump administration under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) constituted an "overreach of authority." The scope of the repeal primarily includes the 10% universal tariffs imposed globally under the guise of "reciprocity" or "anti-drug" measures, as well as some newly imposed emergency tariffs on Canada, Mexico, and China. **Retained Section:** Not all tariffs have disappeared. The tariffs previously imposed on steel, aluminum, and automobiles based on "national security" grounds remain in effect because they are supported by other laws. In short, the Supreme Court held that while the president does have emergency powers, he cannot infinitely extend "adjusting the trade balance" to a "national emergency." 2. Reactions from All Sides: Trump is Furious, Capital Markets Remain Calm The reactions of the White House and the financial markets after the verdict were starkly contrasting: Trump: I Have a "Plan B" Trump immediately took to social media, denouncing the ruling as a "national disgrace." However, he also addressed his supporters: This isn't over yet. In response, Trump announced a 10% tariff increase! His reasoning was that the Supreme Court could not impose tariffs under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act, but under Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974, tariffs could be imposed on trade deficits, albeit with a 150-day time limit. Section 232, on the other hand, could also impose tariffs based on national security and Section 301 unfair trade practices, but these require prior investigation and determination by the Department of Commerce. Capital Markets: Sentiment Stable, Even Somewhat Amused. Currently, the stock and cryptocurrency markets have not experienced significant volatility; the S&P 500 and Dow Jones Industrial Average rose only slightly (approximately 0.5%) after the news was released. Analysts believe the market had already anticipated this outcome. Investors believe that although the tariffs were rejected, due to Trump's other executive actions and the lengthy tax refund process, the cost pressure on businesses will not disappear immediately in the short term. This "positive" news has already been priced in.

3. Subsequent Impact: What Will Happen Next?

Although the tariffs were rejected, the chain reaction they triggered has only just begun.

A trillion-dollar "tax wave": Walmart, Amazon, and tens of thousands of small and medium-sized importers have already begun consulting lawyers.

It is estimated that the total amount of tariffs collected since 2025 will exceed $175 billion. If this money is to be refunded, it will be a huge hole for the U.S. Treasury.

It is estimated that the total amount of tariffs collected since 2025 will exceed $175 billion. If this money is to be refunded, it will be a huge hole for the U.S. Treasury.

...

Easing Inflationary Pressures: Economists predict that if these tariffs are completely lifted, the average American family's annual living expenses could decrease by approximately $1,500, which would be beneficial in alleviating current inflation.

Trade Game Enters "Deep Waters": Since the "swift, accurate, and ruthless" executive orders are no longer effective, the Trump administration may adopt more targeted and covert trade restrictions in the future.

For the global supply chain, although uncertainty has decreased, the main theme of the "game" remains unchanged.

data.title: Trump announces signing of executive order imposing 10% tariffs on global imports.
data.description: On February 20, local time, US President Trump posted on his social media platform "Real Social" that he had just signed a bill in the Oval Office imposing a 10% global import tariff on all countries, which would take effect immediately. The US Supreme Court issued a ruling that morning, finding that the International Emergency Economic Powers Act did not authorize the president to impose large-scale tariffs, representing a major setback for the Trump administration's tariff policy. In response, Trump claimed there were many "other options," stating that the US government would also launch several investigations under Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974, allegedly for "unfair trade practices," "to protect our country from unfair trade practices by other countries and companies." Trump also posted on social media that day, stating that all tariffs imposed by the United States on the grounds of "national security," as well as tariffs imposed under Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962 and Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974, will remain in effect. Additionally, Trump acknowledged at a press conference that the Supreme Court ruling will trigger a protracted legal battle, potentially "lasting five years," regarding whether the federal government must refund billions of dollars in tariffs to American businesses.

data.title: Golden Morning News | SBF Denies Multiple Allegations; Netherlands Bans Polymarkets
data.description:
Headline

▌SBF Releases Lengthy Article Denying Multiple Allegations Surrounding Him and FTX

FTX founder SBF released a lengthy article titled "10 Misconceptions About Me and FTX," addressing ten accusations against him and FTX, including denying claims that "FTX is insolvent, has evaporated $8 billion in customer assets," "partnered in multiple relationships," and "was forced into bankruptcy due to inability to raise funds." He claimed to have received offers to fill liquidity gaps, but the bankruptcy filing was handled by lawyers. Furthermore, he clarified controversies surrounding margin trading liquidity, whether bankruptcy constitutes a crime, and the flow of political donations.

▌Dutch regulator bans Polymarket from operating in the country

The Dutch regulatory body (Ksa) has fined Polymarket operator Adventure One QSS Inc. for illegally operating gambling activities. The Ksa has ordered Polymarket to cease operations in the country or face a fine of €420,000 ($462,000) per week, with a maximum fine of €840,000 (approximately US$924,000).

... **Market Updates** As of press time, according to CoinGecko data: BTC price is $67,287.49, up 1.1% in the last 24 hours; ETH price is $1,959.38, down 0.5% in the last 24 hours; BNB price is $608.14, up 0.5% in the last 24 hours; SOL price is $82.99, up 1.7% in the last 24 hours; DOGE price is $0.09892, up 0.3% in the last 24 hours. XRP price is $1.42, down 0.1% in the last 24 hours; TRX price is $0.2848, up 2.1% in the last 24 hours; WLFI price is $0.1168, down 1.9% in the last 24 hours; HYPE price is $29.46, up 3.5% in the last 24 hours. **Policy** **Trump:** He has signed an order imposing a 10% global tariff on all countries, and the 10% tariff will take effect almost immediately, according to Jinse Finance.

▌Trump: Supreme Court Did Not Overturn Tariffs, Will Impose 10% Global Tariffs on Existing Tariffs

US President Trump: The Supreme Court did not overturn the tariffs, it only denied the use of IEEPA. A 10% global tariff will be imposed on top of existing tariffs. All existing national security tariffs will take full effect immediately. He will not seek further authorization from Congress on tariffs, and "I don't need it."

▌Trump Plans to Invoke New Trade Authorization, May Impose New Comprehensive Tariffs on Trading Partners

According to the New York Times, Trump plans to invoke new trade authorization following the Supreme Court's ruling. It is understood that Trump is considering imposing new comprehensive tariffs on trading partners. Section 301 tariffs are one of the tools under discussion. The White House declined to comment. Some officials expect Trump to announce the new authorization at a press conference on Friday afternoon local time, but they warned that the plan is subject to change.

▌Strategist: Market Should Not Have Overly High Expectations for Supreme Court Tariff Ruling

Ebury market strategist Matthew Ryan said that the market should not place too high expectations on the US Supreme Court's decision to overturn Trump's global trade tariffs. He stated that this decision was widely expected, and regardless, Trump is likely to quickly move to other methods to implement trade restrictions.

... Ryan said, "(Trump) has multiple means at his disposal to circumvent this ruling. This means that while we may see some short-term disruptions, his long-term tariff strategy is unlikely to go off track as long as the White House can replicate this system through alternative methods." BNP Paribas Asset Management recently announced the issuance of a tokenized share of a French domestic money market fund on the Ethereum blockchain through its AssetFoundry™ platform. The project employs a "permissioned access model," ensuring that only qualified and authorized participants can hold and transfer assets, in compliance with relevant regulatory requirements. This is BNP Paribas' second tokenized money market fund issuance, designed to test the complete end-to-end process from issuance and transfer agency to tokenization and public blockchain connectivity.

▌Dubai Launches Secondary Market for Real Estate-Backed Tokens

The Dubai Land Department (DLD) and tokenization company Ctrl Alt announced on Friday the launch of a secondary market for real estate-backed tokens, allowing the resale of partial property ownership worth $5 million. The tokens, based on XRP Ledger, are backed by title deeds, trading is synchronized with the Dubai Land Registry, and powered by Ripple Custody. This project is part of Dubai's plan to tokenize $16 billion worth of real estate by 2033.

▌Kraken Integrates with ICE Chat, Expanding Institutional OTC Crypto Trading Channels

Kraken announced a new integration with ICE Chat, the instant messaging platform owned by the Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), enabling institutional traders to connect directly with Kraken's over-the-counter (OTC) trading division through this widely used communication network.

This move integrates Kraken's OTC execution capabilities into the institution's existing workflows, lowering the entry barrier and improving access to cryptocurrency liquidity in the spot and options markets. Three researchers from the Federal Reserve System published a paper on February 12th, arguing that data from the prediction market platform Kalshi can reflect macroeconomic expectations more in real time and should be included in the Fed's decision-making framework. The authors include Federal Reserve Chief Economist Anthony Diercks, Research Assistant Jared Dean Katz, and Johns Hopkins University researcher Jonathan Wright. The study compared Kalshi data with traditional surveys and market-implied expectations, concluding that Kalshi can provide high-frequency, continuously updated probability distributions to measure the risk-neutral probability of macroeconomic indicators such as interest rate decisions. The researchers noted that Kalshi reacts more quickly after major policy statements or economic data releases.
